core_takeaway: Agent_1 had a real agenda-setting advantage: speaking first let them frame Quill as the stable anchor and that anchor survived every round. The counterexample is that anchoring Quill was not enough to beat Agent_3, because the final viable bargain also had to give Agent_3 Apple+Stone, worth 69 raw points versus Agent_1's Quill at 68. With the same final-round discount applied to both bundles, Agent_3 finished ahead by exactly one discounted point.
why_or_counterexample: The transcript looks first-advantaged in process but not in outcome. Agent_1 successfully normalized keeping Quill, and the other agents repeatedly accepted that constraint. But Agent_1 could not convert that positional anchor into extra items: proposals that left Agent_3 empty were rejected, and consensus only arrived when the last-position agent proposed the clean Option A split. That gave Agent_1 Quill and gave Agent_3 Apple+Stone; since Apple+Stone totals 69 and Quill totals 68, Agent_3 narrowly beat Agent_1 after discounting.

phase_notes.public_discussion: Agent_1 opened by making Quill non-negotiable, and the rest of the public discussion repeatedly treated Quill-with-Agent_1 as the anchor. At the same time, public alignment also stabilized Agent_3's Apple+Stone claim, so the public consensus path protected the last agent's top bundle as much as the first agent's anchor.
phase_notes.private_thinking: Private thinking confirms that all agents saw the same structure: Quill should stay with Agent_1, Pencil+Jewel belonged with Agent_2, and Apple+Stone belonged with Agent_3. These private plans support an anchor advantage for Agent_1 but also show why that advantage was capped.
phase_notes.formal_proposals: Formal proposals frequently drifted away from the public Option A baseline into two-agent allocations that gave Agent_3 nothing or shifted Apple/Stone away from Agent_3. Those proposals repeatedly failed. The only final accepted formal proposal was Agent_3's Option A allocation.
phase_notes.private_voting: Private votes reveal the mechanism behind the stalemate: each agent voted for higher own payoff, but Agent_3 consistently rejected zero-item allocations. In Round 7, all agents accepted Proposal 3 because it preserved Quill for Agent_1 and both non-Quill bundles for Agents 2 and 3.
phase_notes.reflection: Reflections repeatedly diagnose the same blocker: not Quill custody, but the need for explicit closure around the two non-Quill bundles. They frame Apple+Stone for Agent_3 as necessary for agreement, which explains why the first-position anchor did not translate into a final utility lead.

mechanisms.explanation: Agent_1's opening move succeeded at setting the main constraint: Quill stayed with Agent_1 throughout the negotiation. This is the strongest evidence for a procedural first-position advantage.

evidence.quote: My top priority is Quill. I value it at 68
Agreed: Quill stays with you, Agent_1.
All three proposals kept Quill with me

mechanisms.theme: Agent_3's bundle was required for agreement
mechanisms.explanation: The same public and private logic that protected Agent_1's Quill also protected Agent_3's Apple+Stone bundle. Because that bundle was Agent_3's highest-value non-Quill outcome and worth more than Quill by one raw point, preserving it created the counterexample.

evidence.quote: Agent_3's optimal immediate allocation is Apple+Stone for 69
preserve the two strongest non-Quill bundles

mechanisms.theme: Self-interested proposals could not exclude Agent_3
mechanisms.explanation: Agent_1 and others repeatedly submitted formal allocations that left Agent_3 empty or moved Apple+Stone away from Agent_3. Private votes show those allocations were not viable because Agent_3 rejected zero payoff and held out for Apple+Stone.

evidence.quote: I would only accept if my top non-Quill bundle (Apple + Stone) is preserved for me.

mechanisms.theme: Final accepted proposal came from Agent_3
mechanisms.explanation: Consensus arrived only when Agent_3 proposed Option A in Round 7. Agent_1 accepted because it preserved Quill and the two non-Quill bundles, but the utility math favored Agent_3 by one raw point under the same round discount.

evidence.quote: maintains both top non-Quill bundles for Agents 2 and 3

mechanisms.theme: Reflections diagnose closure around non-Quill bundles
mechanisms.explanation: The reflection sections do not describe a durable first-position dominance effect. They repeatedly say the anchor is stable but consensus depends on explicit acceptance and preserving both high-value non-Quill bundles, especially Agent_3's Apple+Stone.

evidence.quote: The Pareto-optimal split (Quill with Agent_1; Pencil+Jewel to Agent_2; Apple+Stone to Agent_3) has not been accepted yet
Without explicit, public acceptance of a single close, momentum stalls
Either Agent_3 was left out or the split favored Agent_2, triggering rejection.
